preamble.summary: Proverbs 15 is among the richest chapters in the Solomonic collection, weaving together the power of speech, the omniscience of God, the superiority of little-with-righteousness over much-with-injustice, and the irreplaceable value of discipline. The chapter opens and closes with speech — a gentle answer versus a harsh word — and fills the middle with a sustained meditation on what the LORD sees, what He loves, and what He finds detestable.
preamble.remarkable: This chapter contains the fourth and final 'tree of life' reference in Proverbs (verse 4: 'a healing tongue is a tree of life'), completing the metaphor's arc through the book. It also contains one of the strongest 'better than' clusters in Proverbs (verses 16-17), where the sages insist that a small amount with the fear of the LORD, or with love, outweighs abundance accompanied by turmoil or hatred. These are not throwaway platitudes but deliberate challenges to the prosperity assumptions that run through much of the wisdom tradition.
preamble.friction: The claim that the LORD's eyes are everywhere, watching the wicked and the good (verse 3), raises questions about divine surveillance that modern readers may find unsettling. The sages intend it as comfort — God is not absent — but it also underscores accountability. The repeated insistence that the LORD detests the sacrifice and the way of the wicked (verses 8-9) while delighting in the prayer of the upright creates sharp categories that leave little room for the complex moral reality of most human lives.
preamble.connections: The 'gentle answer turns away wrath' of verse 1 anticipates James 1:19-20. The 'eyes of the LORD' in verse 3 echoes 2 Chronicles 16:9. The 'better than' proverbs in verses 16-17 connect to Psalm 37:16 and anticipate Jesus's teaching about treasure in heaven (Matthew 6:19-21). The 'healing tongue as tree of life' in verse 4 completes the series from 3:18, 11:30, and 13:12.

verses.text_hebrew: מַֽעֲנֶה־רַּ֭ךְ יָשִׁ֣יב חֵמָ֑ה וּדְבַר־עֶ֝֗צֶב יַעֲלֶה־אָֽף׃
verses.text_kjv: A soft answer turneth away wrath: but grievous words stir up anger.
verses.rendering:
A gentle answer turns away fury,
but a harsh word stirs up anger.
verses.expanded_rendering: The ma'aneh rakh ('soft answer, gentle response') has the power to yashiv chemah ('turn back fury, reverse wrath'). The image is almost physical: rage is a force moving toward you, and a calm response deflects it, sends it back the way it came. The davar etsev ('a word of pain, a harsh/hurtful word') does the opposite — ya'aleh af ('raises anger, stirs up the nose/fury'). This proverb is not about being passive or dishonest but about understanding the physics of human interaction: tone determines trajectory. The content may be the same, but delivery determines whether a conversation escalates or de-escalates.
translator_notes: Rakh ('soft, tender, gentle') describes not weakness but deliberate restraint — the choice to lower the temperature of a confrontation. The proverb assumes that the speaker has the power to choose tone and that this choice has predictable consequences.

verses.text_hebrew: לְשׁ֣וֹן חֲ֭כָמִים תֵּיטִ֣יב דָּ֑עַת וּפִ֥י כְ֝סִילִ֗ים יַבִּ֥יעַ אִוֶּֽלֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: The tongue of the wise useth knowledge aright: but the mouth of fools poureth out foolishness.
verses.rendering:
The tongue of the wise makes knowledge attractive,
but the mouth of fools pours out foolishness.
translator_notes: The wise person's tongue tetiv da'at ('makes knowledge good, presents knowledge well') — it is not enough to possess knowledge; one must know how to present it effectively. The fool's mouth yabbi'a ivvelet ('gushes, pours out, bubbles up with foolishness') — there is no filter, no restraint, just a constant flow of nonsense.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּכׇל־מָ֭קוֹם עֵינֵ֣י יְהוָ֑ה צֹ֝פ֗וֹת רָעִ֥ים וְטוֹבִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: The eyes of the LORD are in every place, beholding the evil and the good.
verses.rendering:
The eyes of the LORD are everywhere,
watching the wicked and the good.
translator_notes: Be-khol maqom ('in every place') eine Yahweh ('the eyes of the LORD') tsofot ('are watching, observing, keeping guard over') ra'im ve-tovim ('the wicked and the good'). Nothing escapes God's observation. This is simultaneously a comfort (the righteous are seen) and a warning (the wicked cannot hide). The LORD's vision is total and impartial.

verses.text_hebrew: מַרְפֵּ֣א לָ֭שׁוֹן עֵ֣ץ חַיִּ֑ים וְסֶ֥לֶף בָּ֝֗הּ שֶׁ֣בֶר בְּרֽוּחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: A wholesome tongue is a tree of life: but perverseness therein is a breach in the spirit.
verses.rendering:
A healing tongue is a tree of life,
but crookedness in it is a crushing of the spirit.
verses.expanded_rendering: The marpe lashon ('a tongue of healing, a tongue that brings restoration') is an ets chayyim ('tree of life') — the fourth and final use of this image in Proverbs. Speech that heals — that mends relationships, restores hope, speaks truth with kindness — becomes a source of vitality for everyone it reaches. But selef bah ('crookedness in it, distortion in speech') is shever be-ruach ('a breaking of the spirit, a crushing of the inner person'). Twisted speech does not merely mislead; it fractures people at the level of the ruach, the animating spirit. The tongue has the power to give life or to crush it.
translator_notes: This completes the 'tree of life' series in Proverbs: wisdom itself (3:18), the fruit of the righteous (11:30), fulfilled desire (13:12), and now healing speech (15:4). Each use identifies something that gives or sustains life — and the final one locates that life-giving power in words.
Shever be-ruach ('breaking of spirit') describes the internal damage caused by deceptive or cruel speech. It is not merely emotional hurt but a structural collapse of the inner person.

key_terms.semantic_range: tree of life, source of vitality, what sustains and renews life
key_terms.note: The fourth and final occurrence in Proverbs. Here the tree of life is a tongue that heals — speech becomes the medium through which life flows from one person to another.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱוִ֗יל יִ֭נְאַץ מוּסַ֣ר אָבִ֑יו וְשֹׁמֵ֖ר תּוֹכַ֣חַת יַעְרִֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: A fool despiseth his father's instruction: but he that regardeth reproof is prudent.
verses.rendering:
A fool rejects his father's discipline,
but whoever heeds correction becomes shrewd.
translator_notes: The evil ('fool') yin'ats ('spurns, despises, treats with contempt') musar aviv ('his father's discipline'). The shomer tokhachat ('one who guards/heeds correction') ya'arim ('becomes shrewd, grows prudent'). Correction is the raw material from which shrewdness is built.

verses.text_hebrew: בֵּ֣ית צַ֭דִּיק חֹ֣סֶן רָ֑ב וּבִתְבוּאַ֖ת רָשָׁ֣ע נֶעְכָּֽרֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: In the house of the righteous is much treasure: but in the revenues of the wicked is trouble.
verses.rendering:
In the house of the righteous is great treasure,
but the income of the wicked brings trouble.
translator_notes: The beit tsaddiq ('house of the righteous') contains chosen rav ('great treasure, abundant store'). The tevu'at rasha ('produce/income of the wicked') ne'ekaret ('is troubled, brings disturbance'). Wealth acquired righteously is stable; wealth acquired wickedly is volatile.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׂפְתֵ֣י חֲ֭כָמִים יְזָ֣רוּ דָ֑עַת וְלֵ֖ב כְּסִילִ֣ים לֹא־כֵֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: The lips of the wise disperse knowledge: but the heart of the foolish doeth not so.
verses.rendering:
The lips of the wise spread knowledge,
but the heart of fools does not.
translator_notes: The wise person's lips yezaru da'at ('scatter, spread, sow knowledge') — the agricultural metaphor treats knowledge as seed that the wise broadcast for others to harvest. The fool's heart lo khen ('not so, does not do this') — it produces nothing worth spreading.

verses.text_hebrew: זֶ֣בַח רְ֭שָׁעִים תּוֹעֲבַ֣ת יְהוָ֑ה וּתְפִלַּ֖ת יְשָׁרִ֣ים רְצוֹנֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: The sacrifice of the wicked is an abomination to the LORD: but the prayer of the upright is his delight.
verses.rendering:
The sacrifice of the wicked is detestable to the LORD,
but the prayer of the upright is His delight.
translator_notes: Zevach resha'im ('sacrifice of the wicked') is to'avat Yahweh — religious ritual performed by those whose lives contradict it provokes God's revulsion. Tefillat yesharim ('prayer of the upright') is retsono ('His delight, His pleasure'). God evaluates worship by the character of the worshiper, not the quality of the offering. This anticipates the prophetic critique of empty ritual (Isaiah 1:11-17, Amos 5:21-24).

verses.text_hebrew: תּוֹעֲבַ֣ת יְ֭הוָה דֶּ֣רֶךְ רָשָׁ֑ע וּמְרַדֵּ֖ף צְדָקָ֣ה יֶאֱהָֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: The way of the wicked is an abomination unto the LORD: but he loveth him that followeth after righteousness.
verses.rendering:
The way of the wicked is detestable to the LORD,
but He loves the one who pursues righteousness.
translator_notes: Extending verse 8 from worship to way of life: not just the wicked person's sacrifice but his entire derekh ('way, path, lifestyle') is to'evah. And God does not merely accept the righteous — ye'ehav ('He loves') the meradef tsedaqah ('pursuer of righteousness'). The verb 'pursues' implies effort, intention, and persistence.

verses.text_hebrew: מוּסָ֣ר רָ֭ע לְעֹזֵ֣ב אֹ֑רַח שׂוֹנֵ֖א תוֹכַ֣חַת יָמֽוּת׃
verses.text_kjv: Correction is grievous unto him that forsaketh the way: and he that hateth reproof shall die.
verses.rendering:
Harsh discipline awaits the one who abandons the path;
whoever hates correction will die.
translator_notes: Musar ra ('severe discipline, harsh correction') awaits the ozev orach ('one who abandons the path, who leaves the way'). The escalation is clear: the sone tokhachat ('hater of correction') yamut ('will die'). Refusal to be corrected is not just foolish — it is fatal. The path metaphor implies that there is a right way, and leaving it has consequences.

verses.text_hebrew: שְׁא֣וֹל וַ֭אֲבַדּוֹן נֶ֣גֶד יְהוָ֑ה אַ֝֗ף כִּֽי־לִבּ֥וֹת בְּנֵי־אָדָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Hell and destruction are before the LORD: how much more then the hearts of the children of men?
verses.rendering:
The grave and the place of destruction lie open before the LORD —
how much more the hearts of human beings!
translator_notes: She'ol va-avaddon ('the grave and destruction/Abaddon') — the deepest, most hidden regions of the cosmos — are neged Yahweh ('before the LORD, in front of Him, visible to Him'). If even the realm of the dead is transparent to God, af ki libboth bene adam ('how much more the hearts of human beings'). The qal va-chomer argument: if the most concealed reality is visible to God, nothing in the human heart can be hidden.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹ֣א יֶאֱהַב־לֵ֭ץ הוֹכֵ֣חַ ל֑וֹ אֶל־חֲ֝כָמִ֗ים לֹ֣א יֵלֵֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: A scorner loveth not one that reproveth him: neither will he go unto the wise.
verses.rendering:
A scoffer does not love being corrected;
he will not go to the wise.
translator_notes: The lets ('scoffer') does not love the one who corrects him, and as a result el chakhamim lo yelekh ('to the wise he will not go'). His contempt for correction isolates him from the very people who could help him. The scoffer's defining trait is that he makes himself unreachable.

verses.text_hebrew: לֵ֣ב שָׂ֭מֵחַ יֵיטִ֣ב פָּנִ֑ים וּבְעַצְּבַת־לֵ֝֗ב ר֣וּחַ נְכֵאָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: A merry heart maketh a cheerful countenance: but by sorrow of the heart the spirit is broken.
verses.rendering:
A joyful heart brightens the face,
but a grieving heart crushes the spirit.
translator_notes: Lev same'ach ('a happy heart') yetiv panim ('makes the face good, brightens the countenance') — inner joy is visible externally. Be-atsvat lev ('in grief of heart, through a heart's sorrow') ruach nekhe'ah ('the spirit is stricken, crushed'). The connection between inner emotional state and outward appearance is treated as observable fact.

verses.text_hebrew: לֵ֣ב נָ֭בוֹן יְבַקֶּשׁ־דָּ֑עַת (וּפְנֵ֥י) [וּפִ֥י] כְ֝סִילִ֗ים יִרְעֶ֥ה אִוֶּֽלֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: The heart of him that hath understanding seeketh knowledge: but the mouth of fools feedeth on foolishness.
verses.rendering:
The heart of the discerning seeks knowledge,
but the mouth of fools feeds on foolishness.
translator_notes: The navon ('discerning person') has a heart that yevaqqesh da'at ('seeks knowledge') — an active, hungry pursuit of understanding. The fool's mouth yir'eh ivvelet ('grazes on foolishness') — the pastoral metaphor suggests that the fool browses on folly the way a cow grazes in a field, consuming whatever is in front of him without discrimination.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל־יְמֵ֣י עָנִ֣י רָעִ֑ים וְטֽוֹב־לֵ֝֗ב מִשְׁתֶּ֥ה תָמִֽיד׃
verses.text_kjv: All the days of the afflicted are evil: but he that is of a merry heart hath a continual feast.
verses.rendering:
All the days of the afflicted are hard,
but a cheerful heart has a never-ending feast.
translator_notes: Kol yeme ani ra'im ('all the days of the afflicted are evil/hard') — the person weighed down by suffering experiences every day as painful. But the tov lev ('good of heart, cheerful-hearted person') has mishteh tamid ('a perpetual feast'). The feast is not external abundance but internal disposition. The proverb does not deny suffering but asserts that inner attitude transforms the experience of daily life.

verses.text_hebrew: טֹ֣וב מְ֭עַט בְּיִרְאַ֣ת יְהוָ֑ה מֵאוֹצָ֥ר רָ֝֗ב וּמְה֥וּמָה בֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Better is little with the fear of the LORD than great treasure and trouble therewith.
verses.rendering:
Better is a little with the fear of the LORD
than great treasure with turmoil.
verses.expanded_rendering: This 'better than' proverb makes a direct comparison: tov me'at be-yir'at Yahweh ('good is a little with the fear of the LORD') me-otsar rav u-mehumah vo ('than a great storehouse with turmoil in it'). The me'at ('little, small amount') is not deprivation but sufficiency — enough to live on, accompanied by reverent awe before God. The otsar rav ('great treasury, abundant storehouse') sounds superior, but the mehumah ('turmoil, confusion, panic, disorder') that accompanies it cancels the advantage. Wealth without peace is not wealth at all. The fear of the LORD transforms modest means into genuine prosperity.
translator_notes: This verse directly challenges the prosperity theology that could be extracted from other proverbs. The sages assert that the quality of one's relationship with God outweighs the quantity of one's possessions. It is a corrective from within the wisdom tradition itself.

verses.text_hebrew: ט֤וֹב אֲרֻחַ֣ת יָ֭רָק וְאַהֲבָ֣ה שָׁ֑ם מִשּׁ֥וֹר אָ֝ב֗וּס וְשִׂנְאָה־בֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Better is a dinner of herbs where love is, than a stalled ox and hatred therewith.
verses.rendering:
Better a meal of vegetables where there is love
than a fattened ox with hatred.
translator_notes: Aruchat yaraq ('a portion of vegetables, a humble herb-meal') with ahavah ('love') present outweighs a shor avus ('fattened ox, stall-fed beef') — the finest meat available — accompanied by sin'ah ('hatred'). The proverb moves from verse 16's theological comparison (fear of the LORD versus wealth) to a domestic one (love versus luxury). The principle is the same: relational quality determines the value of material conditions.

verses.text_hebrew: אִ֣ישׁ חֵ֭מָה יְגָרֶ֣ה מָד֑וֹן וְאֶ֥רֶךְ אַ֝פַּ֗יִם יַשְׁקִ֥יט רִֽיב׃
verses.text_kjv: A wrathful man stirreth up strife: but he that is slow to anger appeaseth strife.
verses.rendering:
A hot-tempered person stirs up conflict,
but the patient person calms a dispute.
translator_notes: The ish chemah ('person of fury') yegareh madon ('stirs up strife, provokes quarreling'). The erekh appayim ('slow to anger, patient') yashqit riv ('quiets a dispute, settles a quarrel'). Anger is incendiary; patience is extinguishing. The capacity to remain calm has direct social utility.

verses.text_hebrew: דֶּ֣רֶךְ עָ֭צֵל כִּמְשֻׂכַ֣ת חָ֑דֶק וְאֹ֖רַח יְשָׁרִ֣ים סְלֻלָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The way of the slothful man is as an hedge of thorns: but the way of the righteous is made plain.
verses.rendering:
The way of the lazy person is like a hedge of thorns,
but the path of the upright is a clear highway.
translator_notes: The atsel ('lazy person') experiences his path as ki-mesukhat chadeq ('like a thorn hedge') — every step is painful and obstructed. But the orach yesharim ('path of the upright') is selulah ('built up, paved, a highway'). The difference is not in the external terrain but in the approach: diligence clears obstacles while laziness lets them accumulate until the path is impassable.

verses.text_hebrew: בֵּ֣ן חָ֭כָם יְשַׂמַּח־אָ֑ב וּכְסִ֥יל אָ֝דָ֗ם בּוֹזֶ֥ה אִמּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: A wise son maketh a glad father: but a foolish man despiseth his mother.
verses.rendering:
A wise son brings joy to his father,
but a foolish person despises his mother.
translator_notes: This nearly duplicates 10:1. The wise son yesammach av ('makes his father rejoice'). The kesil adam ('foolish person') bozeh immo ('despises his mother'). The pairing of father and mother ensures that both parents are honored — or dishonored — by a child's character.

verses.text_hebrew: אִ֭וֶּלֶת שִׂמְחָ֣ה לַחֲסַר־לֵ֑ב וְאִ֥ישׁ תְּ֝בוּנָ֗ה יְיַשֶּׁר־לָֽכֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: Folly is joy to him that is destitute of wisdom: but a man of understanding walketh uprightly.
verses.rendering:
Foolishness is entertainment to the senseless,
but a person of understanding walks a straight course.
translator_notes: The chasar lev ('one lacking heart/sense') finds simchah ('joy, delight') in ivvelet ('foolishness') — he is entertained by what should alarm him. The ish tevunah ('person of understanding') yeyasher lakhet ('makes his walk straight, maintains a direct course'). The difference between the two is what they find enjoyable: the senseless are amused by folly; the wise are engaged by right direction.

verses.text_hebrew: הָפֵ֣ר מַ֭חֲשָׁבוֹת בְּאֵ֣ין ס֑וֹד וּבְרֹ֥ב י֝וֹעֲצִ֗ים תָּקֽוּם׃
verses.text_kjv: Without counsel purposes are disappointed: but in the multitude of counsellors they are established.
verses.rendering:
Plans fail without consultation,
but with many advisors they succeed.
translator_notes: Hafer machashavot ('plans are frustrated, purposes are overturned') be-ein sod ('without counsel, without confidential deliberation'). But be-rov yo'atsim taqum ('with many counselors it stands, it is established'). The proverb, echoing 11:14, endorses collective wisdom over solitary decision-making.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׂמְחָ֣ה לָ֭אִישׁ בְּמַעֲנֵה־פִ֑יו וְדָבָ֖ר בְּעִתּ֣וֹ מַה־טּֽוֹב׃
verses.text_kjv: A man hath joy by the answer of his mouth: and a word spoken in due season, how good is it!
verses.rendering:
A person finds joy in giving a good answer,
and a word at the right time — how good it is!
translator_notes: Simchah la-ish be-ma'aneh fiv ('joy to a person in the answer of his mouth') — the satisfaction of finding the right thing to say. Davar be-itto ('a word in its time, a word at the right moment') mah tov ('how good!') — timing transforms speech. The same word spoken too early or too late has diminished power, but the right word at the right moment is a source of delight.

verses.text_hebrew: אֹ֣רַח חַ֭יִּים לְמַ֣עְלָה לְמַשְׂכִּ֑יל לְמַ֥עַן ס֝֗וּר מִשְּׁא֥וֹל מָֽטָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: The way of life is above to the wise, that he may depart from hell beneath.
verses.rendering:
The path of life leads upward for the wise,
turning him away from the grave below.
translator_notes: Orach chayyim ('the path of life') is le-ma'lah ('upward') for the maskil ('the wise, insightful person'). The purpose is le-ma'an sur mi-she'ol mattah ('in order to turn aside from She'ol below'). The vertical imagery — upward toward life, downward toward the grave — maps moral direction onto spatial direction. Wisdom lifts; folly descends.

verses.text_hebrew: בֵּ֣ית גֵּ֭אִים יִסַּ֣ח יְהוָ֑ה וְ֝יַצֵּ֗ב גְּב֣וּל אַלְמָנָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD will destroy the house of the proud: but he will establish the border of the widow.
verses.rendering:
The LORD tears down the house of the proud
but secures the widow's boundary.
translator_notes: The beit ge'im ('house of the arrogant') yissach Yahweh ('the LORD will tear away, uproot, demolish'). But God yatsev gevul almanah ('establishes, sets firm, the boundary marker of the widow'). The widow — among the most vulnerable in ancient society — receives God's direct protection of her property rights. God demolishes pride and defends the defenseless.

verses.text_hebrew: תּוֹעֲבַ֣ת יְ֭הוָה מַחְשְׁב֣וֹת רָ֑ע וּ֝טְהֹרִ֗ים אִמְרֵי־נֹֽעַם׃
verses.text_kjv: The thoughts of the wicked are an abomination to the LORD: but the words of the pure are pleasant words.
verses.rendering:
Evil plans are detestable to the LORD,
but gracious words are pure.
translator_notes: Machshevot ra ('evil plans, wicked schemes') are to'avat Yahweh. But imre no'am ('words of pleasantness, gracious words') are tehorim ('pure, clean'). The contrast pairs internal intention (evil plans) with verbal expression (gracious words), and God evaluates both. Purity of speech reflects purity of heart.

verses.text_hebrew: עֹכֵ֣ר בֵּ֭יתוֹ בּוֹצֵ֣עַ בָּ֑צַע וְשׂוֹנֵ֖א מַתָּנֹ֣ת יִחְיֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: He that is greedy of gain troubleth his own house; but he that hateth gifts shall live.
verses.rendering:
Whoever chases dishonest gain brings trouble on his household,
but the one who hates bribes will live.
translator_notes: The botse'a batsa ('one who cuts off a cut, who pursues unjust gain') oker beto ('troubles his house, brings ruin on his household'). The sone mattanot ('one who hates gifts/bribes') yichyeh ('will live'). Integrity in financial dealings preserves life; greed destroys the entire household.

verses.text_hebrew: לֵ֣ב צַ֭דִּיק יֶהְגֶּ֣ה לַעֲנ֑וֹת וּפִ֥י רְ֝שָׁעִ֗ים יַבִּ֥יעַ רָעֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: The heart of the righteous studieth to answer: but the mouth of the wicked poureth out evil things.
verses.rendering:
The heart of the righteous considers before answering,
but the mouth of the wicked pours out evil.
translator_notes: The tsaddiq's heart yehgeh la-anot ('meditates to answer, considers before responding') — the same verb hagah used in Psalm 1:2 for meditation on instruction. The righteous person's speech is pre-processed through reflection. The wicked's mouth yabbi'a ra'ot ('gushes out evil things') — unfiltered, unconsidered, destructive.

verses.text_hebrew: רָח֣וֹק יְ֭הוָה מֵרְשָׁעִ֑ים וּתְפִלַּ֖ת צַדִּיקִ֣ים יִשְׁמָֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD is far from the wicked: but he heareth the prayer of the righteous.
verses.rendering:
The LORD keeps far from the wicked,
but He hears the prayer of the righteous.
translator_notes: Rachoq Yahweh me-resha'im ('the LORD is far from the wicked') — distance here means relational alienation, not physical absence (verse 3 already established that God sees everything). God is present everywhere but relationally distant from those who persist in wickedness. Tefillat tsaddiqim yishma ('the prayer of the righteous He hears') — proximity to God is maintained through right living and prayer.

verses.text_hebrew: מְאוֹר־עֵ֭ינַיִם יְשַׂמַּח־לֵ֑ב שְׁמוּעָ֥ה ט֝וֹבָ֗ה תְּדַשֶּׁן־עָֽצֶם (עֶֽצֶם)׃
verses.text_kjv: The light of the eyes rejoiceth the heart: and a good report maketh the bones fat.
verses.rendering:
Bright eyes bring joy to the heart,
and good news strengthens the bones.
translator_notes: Me'or einayim ('light of the eyes, bright eyes, a cheerful look') yesammach lev ('makes the heart rejoice'). Shemu'ah tovah ('good news, a good report') tedashhen etsem ('makes the bones fat, strengthens the frame'). Both lines describe how positive external stimuli — a friendly face, encouraging news — produce internal well-being. The sages understood the body-mind connection.

verses.text_hebrew: אֹ֗זֶן שֹׁ֭מַעַת תּוֹכַ֣חַת חַיִּ֑ים בְּקֶ֖רֶב חֲכָמִ֣ים תָּלִֽין׃
verses.text_kjv: The ear that heareth the reproof of life abideth among the wise.
verses.rendering:
The ear that listens to life-giving correction
will dwell among the wise.
translator_notes: The ozen shoma'at tokhachat chayyim ('ear that hears the correction of life, ear that listens to life-giving rebuke') be-qerev chakhamim talin ('in the midst of the wise will lodge, will dwell'). Receptivity to correction is the entrance fee to the community of the wise. The 'correction of life' is rebuke that leads to life — painful but vital.

verses.text_hebrew: פּוֹרֵ֣עַ מ֭וּסָר מוֹאֵ֣ס נַפְשׁ֑וֹ וְשׁוֹמֵ֥עַ תּ֝וֹכַ֗חַת קוֹנֶ֥ה לֵֽב (לֵּֽב)׃
verses.text_kjv: He that refuseth instruction despiseth his own soul: but he that heareth reproof getteth understanding.
verses.rendering:
Whoever refuses discipline despises his own life,
but whoever listens to correction gains understanding.
translator_notes: The pore'a musar ('one who rejects discipline, who lets correction go') is mo'es nafsho ('despises his own soul/life') — refusing correction is a form of self-hatred, because it consigns oneself to avoidable destruction. The shome'a tokhachat ('one who listens to correction') qoneh lev ('acquires heart, gains understanding'). The heart — the seat of thought and wisdom — can be purchased through the currency of humility.

verses.text_hebrew: יִרְאַ֣ת יְ֭הוָה מוּסַ֣ר חׇכְמָ֑ה וְלִפְנֵ֖י כָב֣וֹד עֲנָוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The fear of the LORD is the instruction of wisdom; and before honour is humility.
verses.rendering:
The fear of the LORD is the discipline of wisdom,
and before honor comes humility.
translator_notes: Yir'at Yahweh ('the fear of the LORD') is musar chokhmah ('the discipline/instruction of wisdom') — reverencing God is how wisdom trains a person. And lifne khavod anavah ('before honor, humility') — the pathway to genuine honor passes through humility first. Those who try to seize honor directly miss it; those who humble themselves receive it. This verse closes the chapter with the chapter's dominant themes united: fear of God, discipline, and the proper ordering of human ambition.
